text: The surgeon uses two O-vicryl sutures on a CT-one needle, each cut to six inches, to perform the sutured closure of the renal capsule.
The surgeon secures all sutures on the renal capsule with a knot and applies a hemlock clip to ensure stability.
The surgeon passes the suture through the renal capsule, preparing for closure.
The surgeon pulls the suture through the renal capsule until the hemlock clip is visibly anchored on the capsule surface.
The surgeon passes the suture through the renal capsule on the opposite side of the defect, preparing for closure.
The assistant applies a large hemlock clip to the suture positioned in the middle of the renal capsule.
The console surgeon uses the sliding hemlock clip technique to slide the clip down the suture, approximating the capsular edges to close the defect in the renal capsule.
The surgeon places additional interrupted sutures across the renal capsule defect, distributing tension to aid in closing the defect.
The surgeon holds the suture perpendicular to the renal capsule while sliding a clip down the suture, ensuring the suture does not tear through the renal parenchyma.
The surgeon closes the upper pole defect using the sliding hemlock clip technique, approximating the capsular edges with sutures and clips to ensure closure.
The surgeon places the most challenging stitches first, positioning the suture to exit at a location that facilitates the assistant's placement of the clip.
The surgeon places the initial suture using a left-handed and back-handed technique, ensuring precise placement within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses previously placed capsular sutures to retract the kidney, enhancing exposure for the next needle throws during the nephrectomy procedure.
